items.passage: Rites of passage are formal ceremonies that mark an individual's movement from one social stage to another. Many cultures use such rituals to guide young people into the demanding responsibilities of adulthood. The events often involve tests of courage, periods of isolation, or the learning of sacred knowledge. Elders typically lead these ceremonies, and the whole community gathers to witness the transformation. Although the specific customs vary widely, the underlying purpose of marking change appears nearly universal.

items.passage: A catalyst is a substance that speeds up a chemical reaction without being permanently consumed in the process. It works by lowering the amount of energy that a reaction needs in order to begin. A small quantity of catalyst can influence enormous numbers of molecules again and again. Enzymes inside living cells are natural catalysts that make vital reactions possible at body temperature. Because the catalyst emerges unchanged, it can often be recovered and then used many times over.

items.passage: Buoyancy is the upward force that a fluid exerts on any object placed within it. A floating object always pushes aside a volume of water whose weight matches its own load. Dense materials such as solid iron usually sink because they displace only a little fluid. However, a hollow steel ship stays afloat because its broad shape shifts a huge mass of water. This simple principle explains why very heavy vessels can safely carry cargo across the ocean.

items.passage: The division of labor is an arrangement in which workers specialize in separate parts of a larger task. By repeating one narrow job, each worker tends to gain speed and skill far more quickly than before. Factories are often organized so that a product passes smoothly from one trained hand to the next. This method usually raises output, yet it can make individual jobs feel repetitive and rather dull. Economists have long argued that such specialization is a major source of a nation's growing wealth.

items.passage: Clouds form when warm, moist air rises, gradually cools, and releases its invisible burden of water vapor. The cooling vapor slowly condenses around tiny floating specks of dust, smoke, and ocean salt. Millions of these minute droplets gather closely together until they appear as a drifting white cloud. Once the droplets grow heavy enough, gravity gently pulls them downward as rain or falling snow. However, the changing shape of a cloud often reveals whether the surrounding air stays calm.

items.passage: An alloy is a metal created by combining two or more elements to improve their most useful properties. Bronze, an early alloy of copper and tin, proved far harder than either soft metal alone. Adding small amounts of carbon to iron produces steel, which is strong enough for bridges and tools. The mixed atoms disturb the orderly rows of the crystal, making the material more resistant to bending. Because their qualities can be carefully tuned, alloys are chosen for nearly every modern machine today.
